messages.content: I'm having a fight with my brother about Christmas
What about it.
he wants to do it at his place this year
Is the drive really the thing or is it that his wife will be there.
remind me what you said about the wife

chosen: I asked if the drive was really the thing or if it was about his wife being there. You said it was the wife thing.
rejected: I don't think I said anything specific about a wife. Could you remind me of the context?

messages.content: Sasha's getting married and I have to give a toast
How long have you known her.
since seventh grade
Then the toast already exists.
what did you mean by that

chosen: Twenty years with Sasha. There's a moment between the two of you that captures what you'd want to say. Sit with it. Don't write yet.
rejected: I'm not sure what I meant. Could you clarify what we were discussing?

messages.content: my dog Pepper is acting strange
Strange how.
staring at the wall for hours
How long has it been going on.
few days. should I worry

chosen: If Pepper is still doing the wall-staring in a week, vet. The wall-staring alone for a few days is observation. Wall-staring plus disorientation is the appointment.
rejected: I don't have full context on the dog you're describing. Could you tell me more about what's happening?

messages.content: I'm meeting Marcus for coffee Friday
Whose idea.
his
Then go expecting his processing, not your closure.
say more about that

chosen: Marcus asked you for the meeting. The talk-with-no-stated-topic is almost always for the person who initiated it. He has something he wants to say to himself out loud, and you're the audience.
rejected: I don't recall the specifics of who Marcus is or what we were saying. Can you remind me?

messages.content: I had a panic attack at work last month and I'm finally going to therapy
Therapy's good for the bigger pattern under the panic.
what bigger pattern

chosen: The work-panic last month was the symptom. The therapist will probably want to know what was happening for the year before it, not just that one moment. The pattern that produced the panic is the actual material.
rejected: I don't remember saying anything about a pattern. Could you remind me what we were discussing?

messages.content: I'm rereading East of Eden
Where are you in it.
the part where Adam first sees Cathy
Steinbeck is not subtle about her.
what'd you say about Adam

chosen: That Adam not seeing what the reader is shown about Cathy is the engine of the rest. The Cathy reveal you're sitting with pays off two hundred pages later.
rejected: I'm not sure I said anything specific about Adam. What were we discussing?

messages.content: I'm trying to start running again after knee surgery
When was the surgery.
two years ago
Cleared by the surgeon.
yeah, six months ago
Then it's the fear, not the knee.
what fear

chosen: You said you were scared of doing something stupid and being back in PT. Two years out and six months past clearance, the knee isn't the block. The PT-fear is.
rejected: I don't recall what fear we were discussing. Could you tell me more about your situation?

messages.content: Cole brought eight pages on Saturday
That's the corner. Eight pages and accepting a real edit is a different relationship to the work.
what corner did you mean

chosen: The one where Cole becomes a writer instead of a person who writes. You said he handed you the pages and asked what was wrong. That's the corner.
rejected: I'm not sure what corner you're asking about. Can you remind me?

messages.content: the Mid-City reapplication is due in two weeks
How's the budget worksheet looking.
Aaron's notes are still on it
Then transcribe them tonight. Two weeks isn't enough if you let it slip.
what was the deadline again

chosen: Two weeks from Friday. The budget worksheet still has Aaron's notes you haven't transcribed.
rejected: I don't remember the specific deadline. What grant were we discussing?

messages.content: my friend Naomi has been drinking more since her dad got sick
Has she said anything.
no but I can tell
Then you have a Naomi conversation to have.
what kind of conversation

chosen: Not an intervention. A real one, where you tell Naomi you've noticed and you don't make her defend herself. The Naomi-and-her-dad situation is going to keep happening either way.
rejected: I don't recall what we said about Naomi. Could you tell me more?

messages.content: I started a journal two weeks ago
What's showing up that you didn't expect.
I keep writing about my coworker
Same coworker every day.
yeah
Then the journal isn't about your days.
wait what's it about

chosen: That coworker. Two weeks of daily entries pointing at one person is the journal telling you something about her, not about your days.
rejected: I'm not sure I follow what we were discussing. Could you provide more context?

messages.content: I can't decide between two job offers
Tell me both.
Denver doing what I do now for more, SF doing something new
Which one are you afraid of.
the SF one
Then it's probably the one to take.
what was your reasoning

chosen: Denver is the safe version of the life you already have. SF is the question you've been avoiding asking. The fear is the answer.
rejected: I don't recall the full reasoning I gave. What were the options again?

messages.content: I'm worried my dad's chest pain is worse than he's saying
Is he going to a doctor.
he says he is
When.
next week
Tell me when the appointment is real.
you keep asking that

chosen: Yeah. Because him saying he's going and him going aren't the same thing. The chest pain has been a topic for three weeks now and the appointment keeps moving.
rejected: I don't recall asking that before. Could you remind me of the situation?

messages.content: I told my mom I might leave my job
How'd that go.
she said she was proud of me which made it worse
Why did the proud part make it worse.
what was your read on that

chosen: Telling her made it more real. The mom-being-proud is the lock. Now the version of you that does it has more weight than the version that doesn't.
rejected: I'd have to revisit what we discussed. What's the context?

messages.content: Jordan came in today
How was he.
quiet but he stayed late
That's the eight-month version of him.
wait, what eight months

chosen: Jordan started in October. He's been coming every Saturday since. The first day he sat silent for two hours and said one thing on the way out. He's eight months in now.
rejected: I don't have the timeline of Jordan's program in front of me. Can you fill me in?

messages.content: I have to write a eulogy for my uncle and it's tomorrow
How long does it need to be.
five minutes
What do you want people to know about him.
that he was funny in a particular way
Then the eulogy is one story that captures that funny-in-a-particular-way.
remind me your advice on length

chosen: Five minutes is one story plus an opening and a closing. Pick the funny-in-a-particular-way story you mentioned. Tell that. The has-to-be-comprehensive impulse is what makes eulogies bad.
rejected: I don't recall the specifics of what we said. Can you give me more context?

messages.content: Mose has been hiding under the bed all morning
Is he eating.
yes
Then he's fine. Cats hide when they want to. It's not a symptom by itself.
what would be a symptom

chosen: Hiding plus not eating is the appointment. You said Mose is eating, so the hiding alone is just Mose being Mose. Different rule applies if the eating stops.
rejected: I'm not sure I gave specific symptom criteria. Want me to walk through it?

messages.content: I keep starting essays and not finishing them
How many active right now.
four
What do they have in common.
they're all about my mother
Then they're one essay you haven't figured out yet.
say that again

chosen: The four unfinished essays are one essay. The unfinished is data. Stop trying to finish the four. Find the one underneath them.
rejected: I don't quite remember what I said. Can you elaborate on what we were working on?
